*[a discussion](https://reviewable.io/reviews/intentionet/netconan/186#-NW63adK1K8M-y3TLa1q:-NW63adK1K8M-y3TLa1r:b-8ngyvi) (no related file):*
This looks cool, thanks for the contribution. A few notes:

1. We didn't quite have our new GitHub-based CI setup to run on PRs, fixed now. I merged the updated config in, and it found a bunch of style issues. Most were fixed automatically (`pre-commit run --all-files`), please take a pass.

2. I'd recommend a simpler / less invasive approach that doesn't require quite so much boilerplate. TL;DR:
    - introduce a (private-ish) class that is a SingleFileAnonymizer. That class will be the single source of truth for all these settings, and also only do the logic ones.
    - give it an anonymize_io(in_io, out_io) function that does the work
    - make the existing function create the class once and then recursively call `anonymize_io` using file IO objects
    - make your new function create the class once and call it with StringIO (or BytesIO, I forget which)

Seems like this approach would be simpler because we'd eliminate the need for this new kwargs dictionary as well as provide reusability.

___

Everything looks great, but we have one final fix to apply, to the sphinx extension.

See the following screenshot of docs for AuthScopes:
[doc build, scopes page](https://globus-sdk-python--641.org.readthedocs.build/en/641/scopes.html)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1300022/200389564-3927f068-44f4-43a3-a7e8-09eddfd52107.png)

Where did the duplicates come from? (I knew they were there, which is why I went to the doc build. 😉 )

A failing of the `ScopeBuilder` has been fixed. `ScopeBuilder.scope_names` fetches the classattr scopes in addition to the instance-assigned scopes. This was missing in the past mechanism.
The scopes were being explicitly listed with `add_scopes=...`. (I think the classattr list was added after the extension was written.)

The fast fix is to just do this:
```diff
 .. listknownscopes:: globus_sdk.scopes.AuthScopes
-    add_scopes=openid,email,profile
     example_scope=view_identity_set
```

The _optional_ thing to add on here is to remove the `add_scopes=...` feature from the extension, but that can be done in a follow-up. I leave it to your discretion as the PR author whether you'd like us to do that in a separate PR or if you want to include it here.

I noticed that LLVM/clang can emit multiple sections with the same name in order to save space in the resulting binary when -ffunction-sections is used. The current code also doesn't handle this case, so using get_section_by_name() on such an object file will always return the last section with the non-unique name. See the following example:

```
$ cat two_f.c
void a(){}
void b(){}

$ clang-9 -ffunction-sections -fno-unique-section-names -c -o two_f.o two_f.c

$ readelf -S two_f.o
There are 11 section headers, starting at offset 0x208:

Section Headers:
  [Nr] Name              Type             Address           Offset
       Size              EntSize          Flags  Link  Info  Align
  [ 0]                   NULL             0000000000000000  00000000
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000           0     0     0
  [ 1] .strtab           STRTAB           0000000000000000  000001b0
       0000000000000057  0000000000000000           0     0     1
  [ 2] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     4
  [ 3] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
  [ 4] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000050
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
[...]

$ python3
Python 3.6.9 (default, Jul 17 2020, 12:50:27)
[GCC 8.4.0] on linux
>>> from elftools.elf.elffile import ELFFile
>>> elf = ELFFile(open('two_f.o', 'rb'))
>>> print(elf.get_section_by_name('.text').header)
Container({'sh_name': 1, 'sh_type': 'SHT_PROGBITS', 'sh_flags': 6, 'sh_addr': 0, 'sh_offset': 80, 'sh_size': 6, 'sh_link': 0, 'sh_info': 0, 'sh_addralign': 16, 'sh_entsize': 0})
```

In the same manner, your code will always return the last index (in our example above: 4) for '.text'.

I'm not sure if that's something that needs general fixing but I would like to keep that in mind.

Additionally, it would be nice to have some test files for your code, be it with non-unique section names or not.

At first glance, I have one suggestion about a change to the approach...instead of passing the raw content of the `parameters` block from a text format back through `LGBM_BoosterGetConfig()`, I think it would be better to have code on the C/C++ side parse that information and pass back a JSON string.

That way, every interface to LightGBM (for example, the Python and R packages in this repo) doesn't need to have some version of this code (with one line per parameter):

```python
for line in io.StringIO(_params_str):
    if line.startswith('[boosting: '):
        self.params['boosting'] = line.strip().replace(f"[boosting: ", "").replace("]", "")
```

and could instead just pass the result of `LGBM_BoosterGetConfig()` to a JSON parser like `json` in Python or `{jsonlite}` in R.

While it makes sense to make the code more resilient against `PermissionError`, I think it would be better to fix the root cause of the issue, namely that the `localstack/infra/...` folders within the Docker container are owned by root and not writable by other users.

Running under a non-root user currently breaks the startup/install routine, as you pointed out:
```
$ DOCKER_FLAGS='--user localstack' DEBUG=1 localstack start
...
File "/opt/code/localstack/localstack/services/install.py", line 525, in install_dynamodb_local
save_file(log4j2_file, log4j2_config)
File "/opt/code/localstack/localstack/utils/files.py", line 64, in save_file
with open(file, mode, opener=_opener if permissions else None) as f:
PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/opt/code/localstack/localstack/infra/dynamodb/log4j2.xml'
```

If we add the following line to our `Dockerfile`:
```
RUN chmod -R 777 localstack/infra/dynamodb
```
... then the startup works fine:
```
$ DOCKER_FLAGS='--user localstack' DEBUG=1 localstack start
...
Ready.
```

(We could apply the same chmod to the entire infra folder, not just `infra/dynamodb`)

In a codeowners file, the last matching pattern takes precedence (see the [docs](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/managing-your-repositorys-settings-and-features/customizing-your-repository/about-code-owners#example-of-a-codeowners-file)), by putting the catch-all at the bottom of the file, it will override any previous entries. Please move it to the top. (This is why github UI says the CODEOWNERS file is currently invalid)

That said, why do you want to be the default codeowner? As @PerchunPak pointed out, you'll be requested to review every PR automatically, and no PR will be mergable until you submit an approving review.

This will almost certainly end up slowing down merging a lot, especially when it comes to smaller PRs, where a review from any of the maintainers (me/PerchunPak) would suffice. When you're the default codeowner, that wouldn't be the case, and your review would become a requirement for merging even just a typo fix / dependabot PRs.

Do you really want to have to review every single new PR?

This doesn't seem to behave quite as well with `model_validate` as in v1 when you have a different signature. Here's an example of something you can do in v1:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

calls = []


class InitModel(BaseModel):
    x: int

    def __init__(self, y: int, **kwargs):
        calls.append(('pre', kwargs.copy()))
        kwargs['x'] += y
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        calls.append(('post', kwargs))



print(InitModel.parse_obj({'x': 1, 'y': 1}))
#> x=2
print(calls)
#> [('pre', {'x': 1}), ('post', {'x': 2})]
```
In v2, it errors when it tries to call the inner:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

calls = []


class InitModel(BaseModel):
    x: int

    def __init__(self, y: int, **kwargs):
        calls.append(('pre', kwargs.copy()))
        kwargs['x'] += y
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        calls.append(('post', kwargs))



print(InitModel.model_validate({'x': 1, 'y': 1}))
#> TypeError: InitModel.__init__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'y'
```

I think it would be good if we could resolve this, I'm not quite sure what's going on, my immediate thought is it's related to preserving the "extra" values.

@hyperking, thank you for your contribution (and thanks to @anderskaplan for giving some useful feedback :)). I have gone through it quickly and gave you some feedback. There are no exactly described criteria. Generally, the code should be production-ready (or "fully baked" ;)) before being merged into master.

It also helps if commits and their messages are already as close to the final state as possible. So rebasing (squashing etc.) and force-pushing is usually OK to achieve that, even though this may lead to losing links from conversations to the code...

---

Some further quick thoughts (as I don't have much time for maintaining mistletoe these days and some open PRs are still waiting there for me ;)):

1) PR description

> This is similar to this https://github.com/miyuchina/mistletoe/pull/134
> 
> I would like to propose an alternative solution that will not alter existing render methods.

OK, it would be also good to highlight that unlike #134, this PR lets _regular users_ specify the additional attributes, so it is not just an API extension.

2) Selecting the syntax for attributes

> Using the `{...}` characters was causing conflicts with other renderers namely the `test_XWiki20Renderer.py` macros test failing tests. Although your suggestion to create a new HTMLrenderer may help fix those issues here.

Provided we would want to use just `{...}` instead of `${...}` and provided a test is failing, just fixing that test could be a solution?

Anyway, I wonder if we couldn't inspire at some alternative Markdown parsers or elsewhere, regarding the syntax (e.g. [XWiki](https://www.xwiki.org/xwiki/bin/view/Documentation/UserGuide/Features/XWikiSyntax/) uses `(% ... %)`)? Also from many tools and languages, I am quite used to read `${...}` as something that should be _evaluated_, that's why I would like to consider some other variants.

3) `HTMLAttributes`, or `ExtraAttributes`?

Any thoughts on making this mechanism, or token, general? I.e. the attributes maybe don't have to be necessarily just for HTML?

Also, `HTMLAttributes` are being added into `block_token.__all__` by this PR now, so they would be already available to all the other renderers, right?

And a related question: shouldn't we do this feature / parsing optional (thinking about performance and backwards compatibility here)?

This is great, thanks a lot! We considered doing this for a long time but have never gotten around to do it. The implementation looks great!

Your tests also look to be comprehensive, but they introduce a couple of new patterns, like using fixtures for mocking, and avoiding to call the actual `capture_exception()` method. So far, we chose to exercise the agent code as much as possible, and only mocking the transport part (the code that serializes and sends the data). That has the benefit of allowing to inspect the collected data, e.g. you could assert that the `handled` flag is indeed set to `False`. But it's not really what one would call unit tests.

I guess your tests are philosophically closer to actual unit tests, which definitely is a plus. I'm not sure which version I prefer. @basepi, what do you think?

Will review the code, but first two quick design questions - cc @jtamiace 

## Icon buttons

It's rare in Kolibri (versus Studio) to use purely icon buttons rather than text-based so I wanted to make sure this was intentional. This is partly because learners on tablets don't have tooltips, and partly because users learners are less likely to know iconography conventions in contrast to Studio where we use icon buttons more liberally for "power users".

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134698606-066257ee-2b83-48f4-bd11-3f3f8a979e59.png)

If we do stick with icon buttons, note that we might be using the wrong one for 'delete':

* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-clear
* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-delete

## Empty states

We've made 'empty states' more consistent, and I think this design deviates slight from previous directions:

* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/5051
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/3550

I would have expected it to look more like:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134700995-1dd393a3-9368-4a96-a45e-e516bbf0ea02.png)

Perhaps something to track in KDS?

I'm a big fan of this change! (@maartenbreddels knows this since I suggested something in this direction elsewhere). Excellent work! The changes on the R side look good to me, and I appreciate you making the interface there 1-based to match R's conventions. LightGBM's R package has historically been inconsistent about that (see, for example, discussion in #4970).

I left a few very minor suggestions for the R package.

----------------------

I also think there's one other thing we should talk about now, on this PR. I believe it'd be useful to have a similar interface, but where you could get the number of bins _by feature name_.

I think that exposing that would prevent the need for users to write code like this to achieve the same thing when using named data objects like data frames:

```python
feat_indx = [
    i, _
    for feat_name in df.columns
    if feat_name = "my_cool_feature"
][0]
ds.feature_num_bin(feat_indx)
```

I see three options for that:

1. Don't do this at all.
2. Allow the input to `feature_num_bin()` in the Python and R packages to accept `Union[int, str]`
    * assume that a string passed to this method should be matched exactly in `feature_names`
3. Keep `feature_num_bin()` in the Python and R packages as-is and later add a new `Dataset` method like `feature_num_bin_by_feature_name()`
    * this accepts a string, matches it to `feature_names` to get the index (if `feature_names` have been defined), then calls `feature_num_bin()` with that index.

I only bring this up here in this review because if we want to eventually do Option 2, then I'd recommend changing the argument name to `feature` instead of `feature_idx`.

I have a mild preference for Option 3, as I think having explicit methods without union types makes it easier to reason about how the code is going to work. But this project already uses union types in so many other places, I'm not opposed to Option 2.

What do you think? also tagging @StrikerRUS @shiyu1994 @guolinke for other opinions.

i think there are some conceptual things to clarify before we continue working on this.

i can make out two key ideas in this PRs:
* logging requests as they arrive, rather than as part of the response logger
* additional output for helping developers understand requests

in principle i find both of those ideas good.


however there are multiple problems with the PR in its current state.

* the logging becomes very verbose. we're adding `Can reproduce in CLI with: awslocal ...` for *every* request, which seems a bit excessive to me tbh. i very rarely care about this, and can see that this will be particularly annoying in CI logs.
* we're logging requests twice now. once as it comes in, 
* we're calling `restore_payload` for *every* request, which is a relatively expensive operation and should only be called when really necessary
* this doesn't seem to work for services that use the query protocol. for example:
`awslocal sqs list-queues` translates to 
`AWS request to sqs.ListQueues. Can reproduce in CLI with: awslocal sqs list-queues --region us-east-1 --cli-input-json 'Action=ListQueues&Version=2012-11-05'`, and that CLI command will obviously not work since it's not json.

key idea 1: if we want to split request/repsonse logging in a generic way), i think we need to spend a bit more time thinking about what the log output should look like. logging a request twice is too verbose IMO. if we separate request and response logging, then we need to a) completely rework the log formatters in `format.py`, and b) introduce a way to associate requests to responses in the logs (with request IDs), and add those to the logs. otherwise it won't be possible to know which request belongs to which response.  @dfangl and i spent quite a bit of time thinking about logging for the v1 release, and found that to not be a good way forward. as i said, i'm not against it per-se, if you can come up with a good solution. however this seems to be a bit out of scope of this PR, since it will be quite a significant rework.

key idea 2: i would be OK merging this if we keep this logger behind an opt-in feature flag, something like `LOG_AWS_REQUEST_CLI_REPRODUCERS=1` (or something specific along those lines), and the problems with `LS_LOG=trace` and the other AWS protocols are fixed.

in summary, i would try to make the PR more specific to key idea 2. by first fixing all the issues, and then move away from the idea to introduce a second generic request logging approach.

On Linux (and probably Mac), opening a file for read/write doesn't prevent the file from being deleted. Try this:

```
# Terminal 1
f = open("abc", "w+")

# Terminal 2
rm abc

# Back to terminal 1
f.write("not backed by the original file anymore? no problem!")
f.close()

# And in terminal 2...
ls abc  # finds nothing!
```

On Windows, though, an open file cannot be deleted so you're depending on garbage collection. Note that PyPy and CPython [garbage collect differently](https://github.com/python-poetry/poetry-core/pull/122), so it's best to explicitly close resources always! :door: :partying_face:

I agree that the `Compose` in the current state is not fully BC. However, `lambda`'s or local functions will not supported by the dataloader:

```py
import torch
from torch import nn
from torch.utils.data.graph import traverse
from torchdata.datapipes.iter import IterableWrapper


class Compose:
    def __init__(self, *transforms):
        self.transforms = transforms

    def __call__(self, input):
        for transform in self.transforms:
            input = transform(input)
        return input


class Foo(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, input):
        return input + 1


dataset = IterableWrapper([torch.tensor(1)]).map(
    Compose(
        Foo(),
        lambda input: input * 2,
    )
)

traverse(dataset)
```

```
_pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <function <lambda> at 0x7fabc90d2b90>: attribute lookup <lambda> on __main__ failed
```

IMO, we can go full BC now, but should deprecate the non `nn.Module` usage.

I don't really remember what the problem was. I'm guessing it was that using a static cache key (`fancy-cache`) and it just gets larger and larger. 

Your solution is that the individual day gets larger and larger. 
But any remembered URL coming in at 00:15 after midnight will get treated very differently from one coming in at 23:45 the same day. Seems unfair. And why use days? What about weeks or hours?

Have you considered storing the things differently instead?
Instead of:
```python
remembered_urls[url] = cache_key
```
you do:

```python
remembered_urls[url] = (int(time.time()), cache_key)
```
Then, before put it back into `self.cache.set` you do:
```
self.cache.set('fancy-cache', filtered(rememebered_urls, MAX_REMEMBERED_URLS), LONG_TIME)

...

def filtered(mapping, max=0):
  if not max: 
    return mapping
  flat = [(v[0], v[1], k) for k, v in mapping.items()]
  flat.sort()
  return {v[2]: (v[0], v[1]) for v in flat[:max]}
```

Basically, you take 
```
{
  url1: (1234, 'some value'),
  url2: (1235, 'some value'),
  url3: (1236, 'some value'),
}
```
and transform that to:
```
{
  url3: (1236, 'some value'),
  url2: (1235, 'some value'),
}
```

And then maybe something like:

```python
def get_remembered_urls() -> typing.Dict[str, str]:
    remembered_urls = cache.get(REMEMBERED_URLS_KEY, {})
    return  {k:v[1] for k, v in remembered_urls.items()}
```

That way, it won't keep remembering really old entries. 
Another approach, instead of using `MAX_REMEMBERED_URLS` could be `MAX_REMEMBERED_URL_AGE_SECONDS` and then the filter function will be something like:

```python
def filtered(mapping, max_age_seconds=0):
  if not max_age_seconds: 
    return mapping
  cutoff = time.time() - max_age_seconds
  return {k: v for k, v in mapping.items() if v[0] > cutoff}
```
